fields.name: 20. School plays an important role in combating violence and aggression
fields.content-01:
1. Research & Question Framing: Violence at School (30 minutes)

‍

Objective: Build factual understanding of school violence while practicing question formulation.

Activity: Guided Research + Question Design

Students work in small groups (3–4 students).

‍

Step 1: Research (15 minutes)

‍

Each group researches violence and aggression in schools, using provided materials (articles, short texts, statistics, news excerpts).

Suggested focus areas:

  • Types of school violence (bullying, verbal aggression, physical violence, cyberbullying)
  • Causes and risk factors
  • Consequences for students and school climate
  • Existing prevention measures

‍

Step 2: Question Framing Task (15 minutes)

‍

Based on their research, groups must create two sets of questions:

A. Clarifying questions – questions that aim to better understand the issue and reduce ambiguity. Examples:

  • What forms of violence are most common in schools today?
  • At what age does school violence most frequently occur?
  • What role do teachers usually play in preventing escalation?

B. Conclusion-shaping questions – questions that could lead to different or opposing interpretations of the same issue. Examples:

  • Does focusing on punishment reduce violence, or does it increase resentment?
  • Are schools addressing symptoms of violence rather than its root causes?
  • To what extent can schools realistically prevent violence that originates outside school?

Each group briefly shares 1–2 questions from each category.

‍

2. Input Session: Points of Clarification & Points of Information (25 minutes)

‍

Objective: Introduce formal questioning tools used in debate and structured discussion.

Mini-lecture & discussion

Points of Clarification (POCs)

  • Asked when something is unclear or ambiguous.
  • Goal: understanding, not confrontation.

Examples:

  • Could you clarify what you mean by “school responsibility”?
  • Are you referring to physical violence only, or also verbal aggression?

Points of Information (POIs)

  • Short, strategic questions or statements.
  • Goal: challenge reasoning, highlight weaknesses, or introduce doubt.

Examples related to school violence:

  • If schools cannot influence behaviour, why do anti-bullying programs show measurable results?
  • You argue that violence comes from outside school—does that mean schools should do nothing when it happens inside?

‍

Key distinctions

Points of Clarification

  • Neutral
  • Strategic / Challenging
  • Seeks understanding

‍

Points of Information

  • Tests logic or consistency
  • Removes ambiguity
  • Pushes toward a conclusion

‍

3. Questioning Practice: Applying POCs & POIs (25 minutes)

‍

Objective: Practice formulating precise and purposeful questions.

Activity: Question Transformation

Students stay in the same groups.

Each group receives 3 short statements related to school violence (provided by the teacher), for example:

  • “Schools are not responsible for students’ behaviour outside school.”
  • “Zero-tolerance policies are the most effective way to combat violence.”
  • “Violence prevention programs take time away from academic learning.”

For each statement, groups must create:

  1. One Point of Clarification
  2. One Point of Information

Groups present their best examples and explain why the question fits that category.

Exit task (individual)

Students write:

  • One strong Point of Clarification about school violence
  • One strong Point of Information about school violence

‍

4. Reflection & Conclusion (10 minutes)

‍

Objective: Consolidate learning and raise awareness.

Guided reflection discussion

  • Which questions helped you understand the issue better?
  • Which questions clearly pushed toward a specific conclusion?
  • Why is questioning especially important when discussing sensitive topics like violence?
  • How can poor or biased questions distort discussions about school safety?

‍
fields.content-02:
Lesson focus

‍

Understanding school violence through research and learning how the way we ask questions shapes conclusions, with a specific focus on Points of Clarification (POCs) and Points of Information (POIs) as structured questioning tools.

‍

Objectives

‍

By the end of the lesson, students will be able to:

  • Identify key forms and causes of violence and aggression in schools.
  • Understand how questioning influences interpretation and conclusions.
  • Distinguish between neutral, clarifying questions and questions that steer toward opposing conclusions.
  • Understand and correctly use Points of Clarification (POCs) and Points of Information (POIs).
  • Formulate effective, strategic questions related to school violence.
fields.content-03:
Materials needed

‍

  • Short texts / articles on violence in schools
  • Statistics or infographics
  • Worksheets for question writing
  • Flipchart or board for examples
